I tested this myself and found a couple things.

1.) this `file not found` error is sort of expected, and can be worked around in a couple ways. See https://stackoverflow.com/q/24664994/2893090.

2.) when I build this on my mac, I get a bunch of warnings like this

> warning /Users/chad/examples/rust/target/debug/deps/hello-486956f9dde465e5.9elsx31vb4it187.rcgu.o': can't open to read symbols: No such file or directory.

It's true, those files don't exist. But `rust/target/debug/deps/hello-486956f9dde465e5` does. So if I run `symbol-file /Users/cssmith/git/gdbgui/examples/rust/target/debug/deps/hello-486956f9dde465e5`, gdb successfully loads the symbol table and `hello::main` successfully inserts a breakpoint and opens the src/main.rs file.

I think this whole class should be a separate context. If you really need part of the Module class (i.e. some methods of it), I suggest we refactor and create an AbstractModule to reuse code. If you don't have `module` as a parameter to this namespace, there will also be no need for parsing empty files in `imports.py`. The contexts are flexible enough to make them bend to whatever you want to do.

Please also call it `ImplicitNamespace` or `ImplicitNamespaceContext`, that's clearer.

`get_filters` would look like this:

```
get_filters(self, search_global, until_position=None, origin_scope=None):

```

Which place should you think will be good for this exception  to be caught? If we want to catch exception for error in deferred initialization, it won't be possible in lower function stack (self.infer_shape or even further down in self._infer_atttrs or sym.infer_shape) as those are generic functions and not specific to deferred initialization. The actual place where error happens is sym.infer_shape in symbols.py. Here (symbol.py) the infer shape returns none. However,  we can't through an error over here because its not an error and sometimes a symbol whose inferred shape is returned as the symbol can be be initialised later as as part of deferred initialization.

>The reason to check for v4 and prefer it over v2 is to correctly handle the case of one of the few remaining v2 services adding support for v4.  At that point, it is unlikely the service would stop supporting v2, so we'd need to account for the case of both v2 and v4 as available signature versions.

I'm comparing how we've rolled out v4 to services so far.  With the exception of S3 (which has its own special signature version anyways) the `v2->v4` upgrade has been completely transparent.  We just update the signatureVersion to say `v4` in the service model.  I picked a handful of services and flipped them back to v2, and they all still work (EC2, ELB, IAM, etc.).  I suspect every service that once supported v2 still supports v2.  Yet we don't call them out here in this endpoints file.  And I don't think we need to.  If the last remaining v2 service, importexport, flips to v4, I imagine we'd just update this file to say that we should start using `v4` for importexport wouldn't we?  There's no need to specify both signature versions in this file.

After a little more consideration, my thoughts are below. @vitaly-krugl can we discuss when you get into the office tomorrow morning? @oxtopus - I'll run things by you afterwards.

- I like this separation of wheel building and wrappers for building the wheel locally vs. in CI.
- My biggest concern is the same as Austin's, which is keeping this stuff simple. I had separate scripts for release and debug previously because argument parsing seemed unnecessarily complex and  I don't like the indirect relationship of environment variables.
- That said, I'm fine with trying to get this all implemented cleanly. With previous CI scripts we started enforcing that they were implemented in Python since bash can be difficult to read and maintain. I'm not sure if this makes sense here or not.
- The build_manylinux_wheel.sh script isn't really specific to manylinux. It uses that name in a couple places but it is mostly just a bash script for building the wheel and when you run it inside the manylinux Docker container then you get a manylinux wheel.

This is a command-line tool.  If it finds a pair of files on disk that do not pass some requirement (such as having matching numbers of data records), the right thing to do is to print an error message (that mentions names of the files involved!) and exit with a non-zero exit status.  So I suggest something like

```python
sys.exit("Expected equal numbers of requests and responses in %s"
         % os.path.join(directory, name + ".*"))
```

@ghcs27 This is an interesting question. I've been trying to review the history. What I can see is that this lock is used only in one place, namely the start method of the EventListener class. When the events module is imported, one instance of this is created, at line 638 of the events module:

event_listener = EventListener()

The start function of event_listener is called only by the subscribe method of a Subscription object. As far as the events module is concerned, the subscribe method of a Subscription object and the event_listener.start are executed in the main thread. 

There is therefore no potential race condition here. It isn't possible to execute event_listener.start simultaneously in two different threads by importing the events module, instantiating a Subscription object and calling its subscribe method. No potential race condition = no need for a lock. It's redundant.

However, if another application imports the events module, runs the subscribe method of two or more Subscription objects in separate threads created by that application, then there will be a potential race condition and you may have each Subscription object trying to start the event_listener simultaneously.

What I'm unsure about is whether that is a problem the events module should be trying to solve. Put another way, I suspect this is but one of many examples of race conditions that could arise if another application uses threads, but which aren't addresses in the soco modules.

What do you think?

> create here is not a row-level permission, right? In contrast to update or read, it applies only to whole Model classes and not rows, if I'm getting it correctly

For our use cases, `create` permission checks *do* typically need to be "row-level" (in the sense that whether or not creating an instance should be allowed depends on the specific contents of the provided data). For example, we allow an admin for a facility to create a `FacilityUser` [for their own `Facility`](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/51/files#diff-51e21fa50df52b4e8505f7ae33dfad7eR256), but we don't let them create one [for another `Facility`](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/51/files#diff-51e21fa50df52b4e8505f7ae33dfad7eR267). Similarly, an admin for a `Classroom` can create an admin `Role` object [for that `Classroom`](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/51/files#diff-51e21fa50df52b4e8505f7ae33dfad7eR467), but cannot create one [for a different `Classroom`](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/51/files#diff-51e21fa50df52b4e8505f7ae33dfad7eR469) (for which she is not an admin).

> Incidentally do you have an example of row-level permissions with DRF you can link to, for my own edification?

High-level description is here: http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/permissions/#object-level-permissions

And the second example here shows a simple case: http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/permissions/#examples

However, thanks for getting me to look into this, as it seems DRF doesn't behave as I had expected it to (from the documentation) in the case of creation (`POST`). Instead, it seems to be recommended practice to [use something like `pre_save`](https://github.com/kevinastone/django-api-rest-and-angular/blob/00888c990a831f7adaba6ace5fd800b3fe7140e1/example/api/api.py#L30) or validation methods to ensure the data being saved is appropriate. Others seemed to be under the same impression as me, and hence implemented things [like this](http://stackoverflow.com/a/18646798), which won't actually be checked in the "create" case.

> if an instance is required as an implementation detail we can hide that from the dev

I agree that it could be done with passing the data as a dictionary, rather than a model instance, and then we could instantiate a model instance internally as needed (and it will be needed, for running `ensure_dataset`, etc, prior to permission-checking). I'm pondering this along with the suggestions around method naming referenced below, and will come back on that soon.

> For example if there's only a 1% chance that someone will write an error but we write 10000 LOCs, then we'll still expect to see a lot of errors. Can we eliminate or diminish the possibility of an error?

I don't think this decreases the chances of errors all that much; the main source of errors would be devs not calling the permissions checks in the first place (whether for reading, creating, updating, etc). We'll just need to police that, when data is being manipulated within Django views, using code reviews and tests. Data accessed through the HTTP API, on the other hand, will all automatically pass through the permission checks.

The problem with it is that after #1786, it's a noop. We pass the current known `block_number` [to avoid a race condition](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/1786#discussion_r202038379) when using the default behavior for the filters of `toBlock='latest'`, which you get entries for a block you didn't received yet, or that a block gets mined right after you got an "outdated" view of the latest events. But, at this point, the `get_block_number` didn't have a up-to-date block number (only the last on the previous run), so it'd actually call the `getLogs` with something like `fromBlock=x+1,toBlock=x`, which will alreays return `[]`. My proposal is to avoid it processing here, and let the events flow naturally on the first poll, which will get exactly the range _from last polled block+1 to current block_.
Of course, it's an option to just fetch an up-to-date `current_block_number` and pass it here, instead of `get_block_number()` (which is outdated until the first alarm task firing up).
